message.content:
CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T89 (goals:G4) — "Gate DATA/PARITY from unbound sources"
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a60e7a44eaa038405 (branch implement/T89, base 352c1e5)
Go repo — gate incl. `go test -race ./internal/bind/...` via nix develop.
acceptance: Test: DATA from an unbound source reaches NO resequencer (dropped) on a shared multi-view socket — including a DATA frame that VALIDLY decodes under some peer's codec; after an authenticated PROBE binds that source to peer B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.
Worker: pass; commit e876ba0; TEST-ONLY diff (internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go new): the gate already holds from T88's continue-then-drop trial loop; new 3-subtest coverage mutation-verified. Judge whether the test-only deliverable genuinely satisfies the task and whether coverage is complete (DATA and PARITY, valid-decode case, post-bind routing, FEC decoder attribution — the task text says never attribute to any peer's resequencer/fecRecv: is fecRecv non-attribution covered or trivially implied?).
Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Show the new test file diff

content.content:
commit e876ba08d7e4448fb0cd11bd5467c5fff2c2c7f3
Author: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Date:   Tue Jul 14 03:01:08 2026 +0100

    test(bind): T89 — gate DATA/PARITY from unbound sources on shared sockets
    
    Add explicit acceptance coverage for the T88 multi-peer source->peer demux
    gate: on a shared (>1 view) concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY from a source
    with no established binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — even when the
    frame VALIDLY decodes under a real peer's psk (a MAC-less DATA/PARITY body
    deciphers cleanly, but carries no binding authority). After an authenticated
    PROBE binds the source to peer B, subsequent DATA routes to B's resequencer
    only.
    
    Verified discriminating by mutation: dispatching the non-PROBE trial-decode
    (instead of the T88 continue-then-drop) makes the unbound DATA leak into peer
    B's resequencer and fails the test. No production code changed — the gate
    already holds from T88; the single-peer fast path is untouched.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

diff --git a/internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go b/internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..e2fdff6
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,130 @@
+package bind
+
+import (
+	"bytes"
+	"testing"
+
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
+)
+
+// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
+// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
+// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
+// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
+// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
+// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
+// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
+// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
+//
+// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
+// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
+// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
+func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
+	pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
+	pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
+
+	// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
+	// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
+	// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
+	codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
+	}
+
+	t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
+
+		data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
+
+		// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
+		}
+	})
+
+	t.Run("PARITY that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		_, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
+
+		parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: 1, ParityIndex: 0, DataCount: 1, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-parity")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode PARITY under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, peerAP)
+
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("an unbound PARITY frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
+		}
+	})
+
+	t.Run("after an authenticated PROBE binds the source to B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
+
+		// Pre-binding DATA under B's psk from this source is dropped (the gate above).
+		early, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 4, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("early")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode pre-bind DATA: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], early, peerAP)
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("pre-bind DATA established a binding")
+		}
+
+		// An authenticated PROBE under peer B's psk binds the source to peer B.
+		const seq = 7
+		ts := newFakeClock().Now().UnixNano()
+		probeRaw, err := frame.Encode(pskB, frame.Probe{PathID: secondView.id, ProbeSeq: seq, TimestampNanos: ts, IsEcho: false})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeRaw, peerAP)
+
+		bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr())
+		if !ok || bound != second {
+			t.Fatalf("PROBE did not bind the source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
+		}
+		// Drain peer B's reflected echo so a later timeout-read in the suite is unambiguous;
+		// its content is asserted by the T88 acceptance, so we only consume it here.
+		echoCodec, _ := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
+		_ = readProbe(t, peer, echoCodec)
+
+		// Subsequent DATA from the now-bound source lands in peer B's resequencer, and ONLY B's.
+		late, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 9, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("late")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode post-bind DATA: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], late, peerAP)
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("late")) {
+			t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA did not reach peer B's resequencer: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA for peer B leaked into the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		// The pre-bind "early" frame was never buffered anywhere: peer B's resequencer held
+		// only "late" (already popped) and the primary's held nothing.
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("peer B's resequencer held a second frame (%q); the pre-bind DATA was not dropped", it.Payload)
+		}
+	})
+}

    gate: on a shared (>1 view) concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY from a source
    with no established binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — even when the
    frame VALIDLY decodes under a real peer's psk (a MAC-less DATA/PARITY body
    deciphers cleanly, but carries no binding authority). After an authenticated

new file mode 100644
index 0000000..e2fdff6
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,130 @@
+package bind
+
+import (
+	"bytes"
+	"testing"
+
+	"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
+)
+
+// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
+// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
+// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
+// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
+// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
+// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
+// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
+// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
+//
+// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
+// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
+// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
+func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
+	pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
+	pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
+
+	// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
+	// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
+	// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
+	codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
+	if err != nil {
+		t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
+	}
+
+	t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
+
+		data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
+
+		// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
+		}
+	})
+
+	t.Run("PARITY that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		_, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
+
+		parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: 1, ParityIndex: 0, DataCount: 1, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-parity")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode PARITY under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, peerAP)
+
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("an unbound PARITY frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
+		}
+	})
+
+	t.Run("after an authenticated PROBE binds the source to B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only", func(t *testing.T) {
+		m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
+		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
+		peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
+
+		// Pre-binding DATA under B's psk from this source is dropped (the gate above).
+		early, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 4, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("early")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode pre-bind DATA: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], early, peerAP)
+		if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
+			t.Fatal("pre-bind DATA established a binding")
+		}
+
+		// An authenticated PROBE under peer B's psk binds the source to peer B.
+		const seq = 7
+		ts := newFakeClock().Now().UnixNano()
+		probeRaw, err := frame.Encode(pskB, frame.Probe{PathID: secondView.id, ProbeSeq: seq, TimestampNanos: ts, IsEcho: false})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk B: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeRaw, peerAP)
+
+		bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr())
+		if !ok || bound != second {
+			t.Fatalf("PROBE did not bind the source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
+		}
+		// Drain peer B's reflected echo so a later timeout-read in the suite is unambiguous;
+		// its content is asserted by the T88 acceptance, so we only consume it here.
+		echoCodec, _ := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
+		_ = readProbe(t, peer, echoCodec)
+
+		// Subsequent DATA from the now-bound source lands in peer B's resequencer, and ONLY B's.
+		late, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 9, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("late")})
+		if err != nil {
+			t.Fatalf("encode post-bind DATA: %v", err)
+		}
+		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], late, peerAP)
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("late")) {
+			t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA did not reach peer B's resequencer: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
+		}
+		if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA for peer B leaked into the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
+		}
+		// The pre-bind "early" frame was never buffered anywhere: peer B's resequencer held
+		// only "late" (already popped) and the primary's held nothing.
+		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
+			t.Fatalf("peer B's resequencer held a second frame (%q); the pre-bind DATA was not dropped", it.Payload)
+		}
+	})
+}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"crypto/rand"
6		"testing"
7	
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
10	)
11	
12	// twoPeerConcentrator stands up a Multipath with ONE shared socket ("a") and TWO bound
13	// peers keyed by DISTINCT psks: the primary (pskA) and a second peer (pskB) wired in via
14	// the bindSecondPeer helper (standing in for the concentrator's per-peer wiring). It
15	// returns the bind plus each peer, whose per-(peer,path) view of the shared socket is what
16	// the T88 source->peer demux routes between. m.paths[0] is the primary's view — the value
17	// the Bind-owned readLoop hands demuxInbound for this socket.
18	func twoPeerConcentrator(t *testing.T, pskA, pskB config.Key) (m *Multipath, primary, second *peerState) {
19		t.Helper()
20		clk := newFakeClock()
21		m, _, _ = newProbingMultipath(t, loopbackPaths(1), pskA, clk) // one shared path "a"
22		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
23			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
24		}
25		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
26		primary = m.peerState
27		second = bindSecondPeer(t, m, "peer-b", pskB, clk)
28		if peerPathByName(second, "a") == nil {
29			t.Fatalf("second peer has no view of shared path 'a': %v", pathNamesOfPeer(second))
30		}
31		return m, primary, second
32	}
33	
34	// TestConcentratorBindsSourceToPeerViaAuthenticatedProbe is the T88 acceptance: with two
35	// peers bound to one shared socket under distinct psks, an inbound PROBE from a fresh
36	// source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec and, on the first MAC
37	// that verifies, the source is bound to THAT peer (source->peer) and its probe reflected.
38	// Only an authenticated PROBE binds; a forged frame verifies under no psk and binds
39	// nothing; the trial-decode stops at the first matching psk.
40	func TestConcentratorBindsSourceToPeerViaAuthenticatedProbe(t *testing.T) {

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"io"
5		"net/netip"
6		"testing"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/reseq"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
14	)
15	
16	// bindSecondPeer constructs a SECOND peerState over the SAME shared sockets an already-Open
17	// Multipath holds, and binds it into m.peers/m.peersByName. It stands in for the concentrator's
18	// per-peer wiring (a later G4 task) so this test can exercise the runtime shared-path fan-out
19	// while >=2 peerStates are bound: it gives the peer its own DynamicScheduler, its own probe
20	// initiator factory + boot probers (aligned with the durable membership m.defs), and a
21	// peerPathState VIEW over each currently-bound shared socket. It must run after m.Open.
22	func bindSecondPeer(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, name string, psk config.Key, clk telemetry.Clock) *peerState {
23		t.Helper()
24		lg, err := log.New("error", io.Discard)
25		if err != nil {
26			t.Fatalf("build logger: %v", err)
27		}
28		cfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
29			LossWindow: 0,
30			Liveness:   telemetry.LivenessConfig{DownAfter: testProbeDownAfter, UpAfterSuccesses: testProbeUpSucc},
31		}
32		// A DISTINCT probe session id from the primary's: a concentrator peer runs its own probe
33		// session, and it proves the fan-out mints genuinely per-peer probers (not shared handles).
34		const secondPeerSessionID uint64 = 0x0123456789ABCDEF
35		newProber := func(pname string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
36			return telemetry.NewProber(pname, id, secondPeerSessionID, psk, cfg, clk, lg)
37		}
38	
39		m.mu.Lock()
40		defer m.mu.Unlock()
41	
42		// Boot probers aligned with the durable membership (m.defs), stamped with each shared
43		// socket's path-id so DATA and PROBE agree on the wire, exactly as Open does for the primary.
44		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(m.defs))
45		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(m.defs))
46		byName := make(map[string]*telemetry.Prober, len(m.defs))
47		for i := range m.defs {
48			var id uint8
49			for _, sp := range m.shared {
50				if sp.name == m.defs[i].Name {
51					id = sp.id
52				}
53			}
54			probers[i] = newProber(m.defs[i].Name, id)
55			health[i] = probers[i]
56			byName[m.defs[i].Name] = probers[i]
57		}
58		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup(health, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Hour}, clk, lg)
59		if err != nil {
60			t.Fatalf("build second-peer scheduler: %v", err)
61		}
62		// Build the peer through the SAME seam production uses (newPeerState), so its Reflector
63		// and every codec derive from THIS peer's psk (T84) rather than being hand-wired.
64		p := newPeerState(name, psk, scheduler, newProber, probers)
65		sendCodec, err := p.newCodec()
66		if err != nil {
67			t.Fatalf("build second-peer send codec: %v", err)
68		}
69		p.sendCodec = sendCodec
70		// A per-peer receive resequencer, exactly as Open Stores for the primary — the
71		// concentrator's per-peer wiring (a later G4 task) builds this; the helper stands in
72		// so handleInbound can route this peer's DATA stream into its OWN buffer and the
73		// engine-facing drainer can release it under this peer's virtual endpoint.
74		p.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
75		// A peerPathState VIEW over each bound shared socket, index-aligned with m.shared (which,
76		// with no deferred paths, is also aligned with m.defs) so the peer's scheduler and paths
77		// stay index-aligned exactly as the primary's do.
78		for _, sp := range m.shared {
79			codec, cerr := p.newCodec()
80			if cerr != nil {

content.text:
Both mutants verified. Evidence complete.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T89 round 1 (diff 352c1e5..e876ba0, test-only: `internal/bind/unbound_data_gate_test.go`); ran `go test -race -count=1 ./internal/bind/...` (green) in the worktree; independently mutation-tested the demux gate in an isolated scratchpad clone (two mutants).
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 1 criticism / 0 questions.
- **Discovered:** DATA coverage is genuinely discriminating — a dispatch-without-bind leak mutant in `demuxInbound` fails subtests 1 and 3, and a bind-on-non-PROBE mutant fails all three. But the PARITY subtest **passes** under the dispatch-without-bind leak mutant: the fixture runs FEC-off (`newProbingMultipath` passes `fecCfg=nil` to `NewMultipath`; `bindSecondPeer` never stores a `fecRecv`), so a leaked PARITY is dropped inside `dispatchInbound` at multipath.go:1260 (`pr.fecRecv.Load() == nil`) regardless of the gate. The subtest's resequencer assertions are vacuous for PARITY; only its no-binding assertion has bite. `fecRecv` non-attribution is structurally implied (the gate sits upstream of `dispatchInbound`, the sole path to `fecRecv.offer`, and is kind-agnostic) but is NOT operationally covered, and the commit's "mutation-verified" claim holds for DATA only.
- **Issues:** none — the shared worktree was left untouched (mutants applied only to a scratchpad clone).

```json
{
  "taskId": "T89",
  "verdict": "disapprove",
  "criticism": [
    "The PARITY subtest is non-discriminating for the drop property it names: with the fixture's FEC receive off (newProbingMultipath passes fecCfg=nil; bindSecondPeer stores no fecRecv), a PARITY frame leaked past the demux gate is dropped inside dispatchInbound (multipath.go:1260, fecRecv nil) and never touches any resequencer — verified by mutation: replacing the non-PROBE `continue` in demuxInbound with a dispatch-without-bind leak fails both DATA subtests but the PARITY subtest PASSES. It currently pins only the no-binding invariant. Make it observe attribution: enable FEC receive on both peers (the same seam Open uses at multipath.go:851 to Store a fecReceiver) and assert the leaked PARITY neither reaches fecRecv nor reconstructs into any resequencer (a DataCount=1 parity that would recover a data frame into the resequencer on a leak makes the failure observable through the existing assertions), then re-verify the subtest fails under the dispatch-without-bind mutant."
  ],
  "questions": [],
```
